title: Workers in low-income countries are less exposed to AI
subtitle: Index of occupational exposure to AI from 0 (no exposure) to 100 (full exposure)
source: Author's calculations based on Demombynes, Langbein, and Weber (2025). Note: The occupational icons are AI generated.
visdescription: Chart showing the occupational exposure to AI, by occupation type and income group
keyFactLabel: workers in low-income countries are highly exposed to AI.

scenes.text: Some occupations, such as information and communications technology (ICT) professionals, are exposed to AI in most facets of their work, with an exposure score of 93.

Others, such as subsistence farmers, fishers, hunters and gatherers, are largely not exposed to AI in their work, with an exposure score of 20.[footnote: The tasks associated with an occupation differ in rich and poor countries. The AIOE Index is based on the tasks associated with occupations in the United States. In poorer countries, similar occupations are likely to have tasks that are less exposed by AI.]
Most occupations fall somewhere in between.

Countries are exposed differently to AI because occupation types differ by country. We can see this by scaling the occupations according to their prominence. Here, this is done for the United States. Defining high exposure as greater than 50, the majority of US workers are highly exposed, because occupations such as business and administration associate professionals and teaching professionals are common.

In middle-income countries, occupations such as market-oriented skilled agricultural workers and sales workers are common. These occupations are notably less exposed to AI than many of the occupations common in the US.

In low-income countries, large populations of workers are engaged in subsistence farming, and less than 20 percent of workers are highly exposed to AI.[footnote: These statistics depend on where the threshold for high exposure is set. Regardless of how it is defined, workers in low-income countries are notably less exposed to AI than their high-income country counterparts.]

limitations.scene4: The bundled file has no United States employment-share column; the renderer discloses and uses the high-income aggregate as a proxy.
